questions.question: Constructed as the grand entrance arch for the 1889 World's Fair, this towering iron lattice structure on the Champ de Mars has become the definitive architectural symbol of Paris, France.

Perched on a narrow mountain ridge 7,970 feet above sea level, this astonishing 15th-century Incan citadel remained largely hidden from the outside world until its rediscovery by Hiram Bingham in 1911.

Commissioned in 1632 by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan to house the tomb of his favorite wife, Mumtaz Mahal, this ivory-white marble mausoleum sits on the southern bank of the Yamuna River in Agra, India.
Constructed under the Flavian dynasty between 72 AD and 80 AD, this massive freestanding oval amphitheater in the center of Rome once held up to 80,000 spectators for gladiatorial combats.
Designed by the visionary Catalan architect Antoni Gaudí, this breathtaking, organic-styled basilica in Barcelona has been under continuous construction since 1882.
Famous for its rock-cut architecture and innovative water conduit system, this ancient Nabataean caravan city in Jordan is entered through a narrow sandstone gorge known as the Siq.

Originally constructed in the early 12th century as a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu before transforming into a Buddhist site, this massive complex in Cambodia is the largest religious monument in the world.
Designed by Frédéric-Auguste Bartholdi and dedicated in 1886, this colossal copper sculpture on Liberty Island was a gift from the people of France to the United States.

Constructed out of reinforced concrete and soapstone between 1922 and 1931, this 98-foot-tall Art Deco statue of Jesus Christ stands atop the summit of Corcovado Mountain overlooking Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.
This sprawling hilltop palace and fortress complex in Granada, Spain, stands as one of the most famous surviving examples of Islamic architecture from the Nasrid dynasty.
Dominated by the massive step-pyramid known as El Castillo, this large pre-Columbian city built by the Maya civilization sits in the Mexican state of Yucatán.

Engineered by Joseph Strauss and opened in 1937, this iconic suspension bridge painted in a signature 'International Orange' hue spans the one-mile-wide strait connecting San Francisco to Marin County.
Located on Salisbury Plain in Wiltshire, England, this prehistoric monument consists of an outer ring of vertical sarsen standing stones topped by horizontal lintels, erected around 2500 BC.
Piercing the sky at a staggering 2,717 feet, this neo-Futurist mega-tall skyscraper in Dubai, United Arab Emirates, has reigned as the world's tallest building since its completion in 2010.

Designed by Danish architect Jørn Utzon and formally opened in 1973, this Expressionist masterpiece on Bennelong Point features a series of distinctive gleaming white concrete 'shells' resembling sails.
Carved directly out of a single ridge of limestone on the west bank of the Nile, this colossal monolith depicts a mythical creature with the body of a lion and the head of an Egyptian pharaoh.

Commissioned by the reclusive 'Mad' King Ludwig II of Bavaria in 1869 as a personal refuge, this Romanesque Revival cliffside palace served as the direct inspiration for Disney's Sleeping Beauty Castle.
This ancient rocky citadel rises high above the Greek capital and contains the architectural remains of several historic structures of great universal significance, most notably the Parthenon.

Sculpted by Gutzon Borglum into the granite face of the Black Hills in South Dakota, this massive national memorial features the 60-foot-tall faces of George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Theodore Roosevelt, and Abraham Lincoln.
Built in 537 AD as the patriarchal cathedral of the Byzantine Empire's capital, this architectural marvel in Istanbul is world-renowned for its massive, revolutionary pendentive dome.
Commissioned by Ivan the Terrible in 1555 to commemorate the capture of Kazan, this vivid, geometric cathedral on Moscow's Red Square is famous for its uniquely shaped, brightly painted onion domes.
Commissioned by King Frederick William II to represent peace, this 18th-century neoclassical triumphal arch in Berlin stood stranded inside the exclusion zone of the Berlin Wall during the Cold War.
Dating back to the 9th century during the Sailendra Dynasty, this massive geometric step-pyramid on the island of Java, Indonesia, stands as the world's largest Buddhist temple.

Located in southwestern Colorado, this rugged National Park protects over 600 ancient Ancestral Puebloan cliff dwellings, including the massive 150-room Cliff Palace built under overhanging rock ledges.

Rising dramatically over the Lhasa Valley from the side of Marpo Ri (Red Mountain), this massive, fortress-like sanctuary served as the winter residence of the Dalai Lamas until the 1959 Tibetan uprising.

Serving as the imperial palace for twenty-four emperors across the Ming and Qing dynasties, this massive, walled palace complex in the center of Beijing contains 980 surviving buildings across 180 acres.
Designed principally by Donato Bramante, Michelangelo, Carlo Maderno, and Gian Lorenzo Bernini, this Renaissance masterpiece in Vatican City stands as the largest church building in the world.
Construction began in 1173 on this freestanding bell tower in Tuscany, Italy, which famously began to sink into its soft alluvial subsoil before the third of its eight stories was even completed.
Originally built as a humble hunting lodge, this sprawling, opulent chateau was transformed by King Louis XIV into the seat of absolute political power and courtly life in France.
Carved by the Rapa Nui people between 1250 and 1500 AD out of compressed volcanic ash, these hundreds of monolithic human figures with oversized heads guard a remote Polynesian island.
Piercing the skyline at 1,815 feet, this concrete communications and observation tower in downtown Toronto, Canada, reigned as the world's tallest freestanding structure from 1975 until 2007.
Home to the historic Djinguereber Mosque built from mud bricks and wood in 1327, this ancient trading hub in Mali flourished as a legendary center of Islamic scholarship under the Mali Empire.

Opened in 1914 after a grueling, disease-plagued construction effort by France and the United States, this artificial 51-mile waterway utilizes a system of stepped locks to bisect the Isthmus of Panama.
Housed inside a massive 12th-century fortress turned royal palace along the Seine River, this institution features a controversial 1989 glass pyramid entrance designed by I.M. Pei.
Rising to a symmetrical, snow-capped cone of 12,389 feet, this active stratovolcano southwest of Tokyo stands as an immortal cultural and spiritual symbol of Japan.
This stunning tidal island and medieval Benedictine abbey off the coast of Normandy, France, is completely cut off from the mainland at high tide, reachable only via a winding causeway.
Completed in a blistering 13 months during the Great Depression, this 102-story Art Deco skyscraper in Midtown Manhattan held the title of the world's tallest building for nearly 40 years.
Located on a high plateau in central Turkey, this volcanic landscape is famous for its towering 'fairy chimney' rock formations, ancient underground cities, and skies filled with hot air balloons.

Discovered accidentally by local farmers digging a well in 1974, this massive collection of thousands of life-sized clay soldiers was buried in Xi'an to protect China's first emperor in the afterlife.
Officially renamed Elizabeth Tower in 2012, this neo-Gothic clock tower at the north end of the Houses of Parliament in London is universally known by the nickname of its massive, 13.7-ton main bell.
Also known as the Wailing Wall, this ancient limestone retaining wall in the Old City of Jerusalem was originally constructed by Herod the Great to support the outer courtyard of the Second Temple.

Discovered by a teenager and his dog in 1940, this complex of caves in southwestern France contains exceptionally detailed, 17,000-year-old Paleolithic wall paintings, primarily depicting large animals.

Constructed between 1609 and 1616 during the rule of Ahmed I, this historic Istanbul mosque is famous for its cascading domes, six minarets, and interior walls hand-painted with over 20,000 blue Iznik tiles.

Running for roughly 9 miles along the Atlantic coast of County Clare, these breathtaking, vertical shale and sandstone sea cliffs plunge up to 702 feet into the ocean as Ireland's most visited natural landmark.

Built as the futuristic centerpiece of the 1962 Century 21 Exposition World's Fair, this 605-foot-tall tower features a flying-saucer-shaped observation deck dominating the skyline of Seattle, Washington.
Dominated by a massive, 660-foot-tall column of sheer rock, this ancient fortress complex in Sri Lanka features a gateway carved into the shape of an enormous lion, complete with massive stone paws.

Commissioned by Napoleon in 1806 following his victory at Austerlitz, this monumental neoclassical arch stands proudly at the center of the Place Charles de Gaulle at the western end of the Champs-Élysées.

Comprising a vast mix of decayed temples, chapels, and pylons, this massive open-air complex near Luxor, Egypt, is dominated by the awe-inspiring Great Hypostyle Hall, which contains 134 colossal sandstone columns.

Spelling out its name in 45-foot-tall white capital letters across Mount Lee in the Santa Monica Mountains, this landmark was originally erected in 1923 as a temporary advertisement for a local real estate development.
Located at 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue in Washington, D.C., this iconic building serves as the official residence and workplace of the President of the United States.
Visible from many aerial and satellite views, this ancient stone-and-brick fortification stretches thousands of miles across the northern borders of China to protect against historic invasions.
Carved out over millions of years by the Colorado River in Arizona, this immense, colorful, and deep natural gorge stands as one of the world's most famous natural wonders.
Straddling the border between New York state and Ontario, Canada, this massive and thunderous group of three waterfalls is a world-famous destination for sightseers and boat tours.

Nicknamed 'The Crossroads of the World', this brightly illuminated commercial intersection in Midtown Manhattan, New York City, is famous for its massive digital billboards and annual New Year's Eve ball drop.
Built as massive stone tombs for ancient Egyptian pharaohs like Khufu, these three towering structures have stood on a desert plateau outside Cairo for over 4,500 years.
Spanning 843 acres in the middle of Manhattan, this massive, world-famous urban park features sprawling lawns, artificial lakes, walking paths, and a famous zoo.
Located in the Himalayas on the border between Nepal and China, this towering, snow-capped peak holds the title of the tallest mountain above sea level on Earth.
Featuring 775 rooms and the famous 'Changing of the Guard' ceremony, this massive London estate serves as the official administrative headquarters of the British monarch.
Standing on the South Bank of the River Thames, this massive, 443-foot-tall giant observation wheel offers tourists sweeping, panoramic views of London's skyline.
This famous four-mile stretch of South Las Vegas Boulevard in Nevada is world-renowned for its concentration of massive resort hotels, bright neon lights, and sprawling casinos.
This active stratovolcano with a perfectly symmetrical, snow-capped cone stands as the tallest mountain in Japan and a legendary symbol of the country.
Mainly located in Wyoming, this legendary wilderness area became the world's very first National Park in 1872 and is famous for its wildlife and the 'Old Faithful' geyser.
This famous freestanding bell tower in Italy is known worldwide because its weak foundations caused it to noticeably tilt to one side shortly after construction began.
This colossal, 98-foot-tall Art Deco statue of Jesus Christ stands with his arms wide open atop Corcovado Mountain, looking down over the city of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.
Painted in a bright 'International Orange' color to stand out in the fog, this massive suspension bridge spans the opening of San Francisco Bay in California.
This giant stone oval amphitheater in the center of Rome, Italy, was built by the ancient Roman Empire to hold massive gladiatorial games and public spectacles.
Originally built as a temporary attraction for the 1889 World's Fair, this massive iron lattice tower on the Champ de Mars has become the definitive symbol of Paris.
Standing tall in New York Harbor, this colossal green copper sculpture of a torch-bearing woman was given to the United States as a historic gift of friendship from France.
This iconic, white marble palace-like monument in Agra, India, was built by a grieving Mughal emperor in the 1600s to serve as a stunning tomb for his favorite wife.
Though the name technically refers only to the massive 13-ton bell inside, this iconic, historic clock tower at the Houses of Parliament is a universal symbol of London.
Spelled out in massive 45-foot-tall white letters across Mount Lee in California, this landmark is the ultimate visual symbol of the American film industry.
Located on the harbor in Australia's largest city, this iconic performing arts building is famous for its unique roof design, which looks like giant white sails or shells.
This towering, 102-story Art Deco skyscraper in Midtown Manhattan is famous for its open-air observation deck and for being climbed by King Kong in classic cinema.
Located in a grassy field in Wiltshire, England, this famous prehistoric mystery consists of a circular ring of massive standing stones erected thousands of years ago.
This famous, narrow waterway connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Mediterranean Sea, separating Spain from Morocco.
Located on an island in the Seine River, this iconic, medieval Catholic cathedral in Paris is famous for its Gothic architecture, gargoyles, and flying buttresses.
Situated on a rocky island in San Francisco Bay, this notorious former federal prison once held famous criminals like Al Capone.
This famous artificial waterway cuts through Central America, allowing ships to pass directly between the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
As the world's largest art museum, this former Parisian palace features a famous glass pyramid entrance and houses the Mona Lisa.
Bordered by Jordan and Israel, this famous saltwater lake sits at the lowest elevation on land and is so salty that swimmers float effortlessly.
Sculpted into the granite face of the Black Hills in South Dakota, this massive monument features the 60-foot heads of four American presidents.
Often confused with London Bridge, this iconic combined bascule and suspension bridge crosses the River Thames next to the Tower of London.
Hidden high in the Andes Mountains of Peru, this world-famous 15th-century Incan citadel is often called the 'Lost City of the Incas'.
Though mostly demolished in 1989, preserved concrete stretches of this historical barrier still stand as a major landmark in Germany's capital city.
Spanning across northern South America, primarily in Brazil, this colossal jungle stands as the largest tropical rainforest on Earth.
Located on Mexico's Yucatán Peninsula, this ancient Mayan city is dominated by a massive, world-famous step-pyramid known as El Castillo.
This massive, incredibly opulent palace complex outside of Paris was built by the 'Sun King' Louis XIV and features the famous Hall of Mirrors.
Standing tall near Tokyo, this active stratovolcano is Japan's highest peak and is instantly recognizable by its symmetrical, snow-capped cone.
Surrounded entirely by the city of Rome, this tiny walled independent city-state serves as the spiritual headquarters of the Roman Catholic Church.
Running through the heart of Manhattan's Theater District, this famous New York street is globally synonymous with commercial theater and massive musical productions.
Located in the Scottish Highlands, this deep, murky freshwater lake is famous worldwide due to alleged sightings of a mythical aquatic monster.
Built for the 1962 World's Fair, this 605-foot-tall tower features a saucer-shaped observation deck that dominates the skyline of Seattle, Washington.
Visible from space off the northeastern coast of Australia, this massive natural wonder is the largest coral reef system on Earth.
Located inside Vatican City, this massive Renaissance-era church is one of the largest in the world and features a giant dome designed by Michelangelo.
Located near Naples, Italy, this active volcano is famous for its catastrophic eruption in 79 AD that buried the ancient Roman city of Pompeii.
This famous cave complex in southwestern France is internationally renowned for its highly detailed, 17,000-year-old prehistoric wall paintings of animals.
Located in Lower Manhattan, this short, historic street is globally recognized as the definitive geometric and cultural hub of American financial markets.
Connecting Manhattan and Brooklyn over the East River, this beautiful historic suspension bridge features iconic stone arches and a popular pedestrian walkway.
Stretching across the entire northern third of the African continent, this massive, arid landscape holds the title of the largest hot desert in the world.
This massive, rectangular urban park in San Francisco is even larger than New York's Central Park and stretches all the way to the Pacific Ocean.
Located in Tanzania, this massive, snow-capped dormant volcano stands as the highest mountain in Africa and the tallest free-standing mountain in the world.
This world-famous upscale shopping street in Beverly Hills, California, is legendary for its ultra-luxury designer boutiques and celebrity sightings.
This famous neoclassical triumphal arch stands proudly at the center of a massive traffic roundabout at the western end of the Champs-Élysées in Paris.
This world-famous, winding Italian waterway serves as the main aquatic 'street' for gondolas and water buses traveling through the city of Venice.
This historic royal forest in Nottinghamshire, England, is famous worldwide due to its legendary association with the folklore of Robin Hood.
This historic Spanish mission and fortress complex in San Antonio, Texas, was the site of a legendary 1836 battle during the Texas Revolution.
As the principal public square of Venice, Italy, this massive, historic plaza is tightly bordered by St. Mark's Basilica and flocks of pigeons.
Dedicated to the goddess Athena, the majestic ruins of this ancient white marble temple dominate the rocky cliffside of the Acropolis in Athens, Greece.
Located in California's Mojave Desert, this intense landscape contains Badwater Basin, which stands as the lowest and hottest point in North America.
Painted in striking white and international orange zones to comply with aviation laws, this landmark Eiffel-inspired lattice tower stands tall in Japan's capital.
This massive, rectangular central plaza in the heart of Madrid, Spain, is completely surrounded by historic three-story residential buildings with arches.
Serving as the massive headquarters for the U.S. Department of Defense in Virginia, what building is famous for its unique five-sided geometric design.
Constructed along the Atlantic coast of Florida, this sprawling historical launch site serves as the ultimate primary hub for American human spaceflight.
This historic, open central square in Moscow sits next to the Kremlin and features the brightly colored onion domes of St. Basil's Cathedral. What is it called?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Alexey Fedotov on Unsplash

questions.question: This iconic, active volcano on the island of Sicily stands as the tallest active volcano in Europe and is in a near-constant state of activity.
This ancient limestone retaining wall in the Old City of Jerusalem is the holiest site where Jews are permitted to freely gather and pray.
This historic cobblestone public square in the center of Prague's Old Town features a world-famous 15th-century Astronomical Clock.
Stretching across fifteen blocks of Hollywood Boulevard in California, this landmark sidewalk features thousands of five-pointed terrazzo and brass stars.
This spectacular, giant marble fountain in Rome is world-famous for the tourist tradition of throwing a coin over your shoulder to ensure a return trip.
Located inside the Apostolic Palace in Vatican City, this historic chapel is world-famous for its magnificent ceiling frescoes painted by Michelangelo.
Spanning the southern tip of Florida, this massive, subtropical marshland wilderness is famous for its unique ecosystem and wild alligators.
This historic complex of commercial buildings in Midtown Manhattan is famous for its massive sunken plaza, ice rink, and annual giant Christmas tree.
What famous landmark is depicted in this photo?
questions.imageCredit: Photo by Mathew Schwartz on Unsplash

questions.question: What ancient city carved into rose-red cliffs is shown here?

questions.question: What ancient temple complex is depicted in this photo?
Known as the 'Lotus Temple,' this stunning flower-shaped Bahá'í House of Worship in New Delhi, India, is one of the most visited buildings in the world.
This enormous sandstone monolith rising 1,142 feet above the surrounding desert in Australia's Northern Territory is sacred to the Anangu Aboriginal people.
Straddling the border of Zambia and Zimbabwe, this waterfall on the Zambezi River is the largest in the world by total area of falling water.
At 9,573 feet, this is the highest mountain in Greece and, in ancient Greek religion, the home of the twelve Olympian gods.
This ancient Roman city in southern Italy was catastrophically buried under volcanic ash and pumice following the eruption of a nearby volcano in 79 AD, preserving it nearly intact.
Hidden deep in the rainforests of northern Guatemala, this abandoned ancient city was once one of the most powerful kingdoms of the Maya civilization.
Home to the Pyramid of the Sun and the Pyramid of the Moon, this massive pre-Columbian city northeast of Mexico City was once one of the largest cities in the ancient world.
At over 4,000 square miles, this immense salt flat in the Bolivian Andes is the world's largest, and during the rainy season a thin layer of water turns it into the world's largest natural mirror.
This UNESCO World Heritage site in Croatia is famous for its chain of terraced lakes interconnected by waterfalls, with vivid turquoise and green colors created by minerals and microorganisms.
Straddling the border of France and Italy at 15,774 feet, this is the highest peak in the Alps and in all of Western Europe.
Opened in 1869 and connecting the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea, this 120-mile Egyptian waterway dramatically shortened shipping routes between Europe and Asia.
This volcanic archipelago in the Pacific Ocean, belonging to Ecuador, was visited by Charles Darwin in 1835 and inspired his theory of natural selection through its unique wildlife.
Though slightly shorter than the Nile, this South American river carries more water than any other river on Earth and flows through the world's largest rainforest.
Straddling the border of Peru and Bolivia at an elevation of 12,507 feet, this is the world's highest navigable lake and the largest lake in South America by surface area.
This flat-topped mountain overlooking Cape Town, South Africa, is one of the New Seven Wonders of Nature and is a globally recognized symbol of the city.

Located on Navajo land near Page, Arizona, this slot canyon is famous for its wave-like structure and narrow passages through flowing sandstone, and is one of the most photographed canyons in the American Southwest.
This famous geyser in Yellowstone National Park earned its name from its consistent eruption schedule, shooting boiling water up to 184 feet in the air roughly every 60 to 110 minutes.
This region of the Colorado Plateau on the Navajo Nation border of Utah and Arizona is famous for its iconic red-rock buttes and mesas that have appeared in countless Western films.

Located in southern Utah, this national park is known for its hoodoos — tall, thin spires of rock formed by erosion — and for having the world's largest concentration of these unique geological formations.
Located in eastern Utah, this national park contains over 2,000 natural sandstone arches, including the famous Delicate Arch which appears on Utah's license plate.
This national park in southwestern Utah is known for its steep red cliffs, The Narrows slot canyon, and Angels Landing — a trail that requires navigating chains bolted into sheer rock.
Formed by the collapse of a volcano around 5,700 years ago, this lake in Oregon is the deepest lake in the United States at 1,943 feet and is renowned for its startling deep blue color.

Located in southern Chilean Patagonia, this national park is named for the three distinctive granite towers that dominate its skyline and is widely considered one of the most beautiful places on Earth.
Spanning the border of Argentina and Brazil, this system of 275 individual waterfalls is wider than Niagara Falls and Victoria Falls combined, and is set within a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Located on the northeastern coast of Northern Ireland, this area of about 40,000 interlocking basalt columns — most of them hexagonal — was formed by an ancient volcanic eruption.
This stretch of coastline along the southern edge of Italy's Sorrentine Peninsula is famous for its dramatic cliffs, pastel-colored towns perched above the sea, and UNESCO World Heritage status.
These iconic chalk cliffs on the English coast near the Strait of Dover reach heights of up to 350 feet and have long served as a symbol of Britain due to their visibility from France.
This string of five colorful cliff-hanging villages along a rugged stretch of the Italian Riviera coastline — connected by hiking trails and a railway — is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
These long, narrow inlets carved by glaciers along the west coast of Norway — including the famous Geirangerfjord and Nærøyfjord — are listed as UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
Located in northeastern Vietnam, this UNESCO World Heritage site is famous for its emerald waters and thousands of towering limestone islands topped with rainforest.
Located in Siberia, Russia, this is the world's deepest lake at 5,387 feet and holds approximately 20% of the world's unfrozen surface fresh water.
Located in southwestern Turkey, this natural site consists of terraced hot spring pools and white travertine terraces that cascade down a hillside, adjacent to the ancient ruins of Hierapolis.

This Chinese national park in Hunan Province features thousands of towering sandstone pillar formations — widely recognized as the inspiration for the floating Hallelujah Mountains in the film Avatar.
Formally known as Rokuon-ji, this Zen Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan, is famous for its top two floors being completely covered in gold leaf, reflecting beautifully in the surrounding pond.
This complex of religious buildings in Beijing, China, was visited by emperors of the Ming and Qing dynasties to perform annual ceremonies of prayer to Heaven for a good harvest.

Considered the spiritual capital of India, this ancient city on the banks of the Ganges River is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world and draws millions of Hindu pilgrims each year.
Located in Myanmar, this ancient capital contains more than 3,500 Buddhist temples, pagodas, and monasteries scattered across a vast archaeological plain.
Located in New Zealand's King Country region, these limestone caves are renowned for their glowworm populations that illuminate the cavern ceilings like a starry sky.
This scenic coastal drive in Victoria, Australia, stretches 151 miles and is home to the Twelve Apostles — a collection of limestone stacks rising from the Southern Ocean.
This rugged Scottish island off the northwest coast of Scotland is famous for its dramatic landscapes including the Quiraing, Old Man of Storr, and Fairy Pools.
This glacial lake in Slovenia, set against the Julian Alps, features a small island with a baroque church and a medieval castle perched on a cliff above the shoreline.
This table-top mountain sits at the tri-border point of Venezuela, Brazil, and Guyana, and inspired Sir Arthur Conan Doyle's novel 'The Lost World.' It is perpetually shrouded in cloud.
Located off the coast of Belize, this giant marine sinkhole — roughly 1,000 feet wide and 400 feet deep — is one of the world's top scuba diving destinations.
This national park in China's Sichuan province is renowned for its multi-level waterfalls and colorful lakes ranging from turquoise to deep blue.
